title: Controlled Vocabulary and Ontologies
text:
Code
import requests
import pandas as pd

CT = "https://clinicaltables.nlm.nih.gov/api/"
FHIR_URL = "https://snowstorm-training.snomedtools.org/fhir/ValueSet/$expand"

# fails without permision API key
#FHIR_URL = "https://snowstorm.snomedtools.org/fhir/ValueSet/$expand"

title: Controlled Vocabulary and Ontologies
text:
Controlled Vocabulary
Below you will explore some of the commonly used controlled vocabularies in biomedical data: ICD-10 for diseases, LOINC for procedures, …
For each of these vocabularies there are also different dedicated DB.
Here I use NLM Clinical Tables because it provides a simple unified interface: NLM Clinical Tables.

Diseases (ICD-10)

ICD history (WHO)
ICD-10 @ clinicaltables documentation



Code
url = CT + "icd10cm/v3/search"

term = "vitamin D deficiency"
# search for {term} in search fields sf: "code" and "name"
params = {"sf": "code,name", "terms": term, "maxList": 100}

r = requests.get(url, params=params)
r.raise_for_status()
print(f"Request Status: {r.status_code}.")

data = r.json()
n = data[0]                # number of matches
df = pd.DataFrame(data[3]) # convert list to df

print(f"{n} matches for term: {term}:")
df.head()


Request Status: 200.
24 matches for term: vitamin D deficiency:









0
1




0
E55.9
Vitamin D deficiency, unspecified


1
D51.0
Vitamin B12 deficiency anemia due to intrinsic...


2
D51.1
Vitamin B12 deficiency anemia due to selective...


3
D51.3
Other dietary vitamin B12 deficiency anemia


4
D51.8
Other vitamin B12 deficiency anemias









Procedures (LOINC)

LOINC @ clinicaltables documentation



Code
url = CT + "loinc_items/v3/search"

term = "blood glucose test"
params = {
    "terms": term,
    "df": "LOINC_NUM,LONG_COMMON_NAME,SHORTNAME",
    "maxList": 200
}

data = requests.get(url, params=params).json()
pd.DataFrame(data[3]).head()









0
1
2

5914-7
Glucose [Presence] in Blood by Test strip
Glucose Bld Ql Strip


1
2341-6
Glucose [Mass/volume] in Blood by Test strip m...
Glucose Bld Manual Strip-mCnc


2
2340-8
Glucose [Mass/volume] in Blood by Automated te...
Glucose Bld Strip.auto-mCnc


3
72516-8
Glucose [Moles/volume] in Blood by Automated t...
Glucose Bld Strip.auto-sCnc


4
104875-0
Hematocrit [Volume Fraction] of Blood --30 min...
Hct 30M p Glc VFr Bld













ImportantCV Tasks




With ICD-10:

search for “sickle cell”, “influenza”, “type 2 diabetes”, or a disease of your choice
How many matched ICD-10 codes does this contain?

With LOINC:

How many different “chest Xray” are recorded in LOINC?
Which gene is tested for “Glu6Val” mutation?

title: Controlled Vocabulary and Ontologies
text:
Ontologies
Large-scale effort to represent medical knowledge.

Snomed Browser
Snowstorm-Training

Here you will explore programmatic requests to Snowmed CT and ontology relationships.

FHIR queries

Search for ConceptID
Example: Identify the Snomed ID for the disorder concept “diabetes mellitus”


Code
concept = "diabetes mellitus"
params = {
    "url": "http://snomed.info/sct?fhir_vs",
    "filter": concept,
    "count": 20
}

r = requests.get(FHIR_URL, params=params)
r.raise_for_status()
results = r.json()["expansion"]["contains"]
pd.DataFrame(results).head()









system
code
display




0
http://snomed.info/sct
73211009
DM - Diabetes mellitus


1
http://snomed.info/sct
160303001
Family history of diabetes mellitus


2
http://snomed.info/sct
161445009
H/O: diabetes mellitus


3
http://snomed.info/sct
46635009
Diabetes mellitus type I


4
http://snomed.info/sct
44054006
Type II diabetes mellitus









Query ECL Template
Snomed CT codes form a large ontology. Queries into this data and be formulated with a so called Expression Constraint Language (ECL).


Code
def query_ecl(ecl_expression):
    """
    Sends ECL query to FHIR endpoint
    Returns list of concepts names     
    """
    params = {
        "url": f"http://snomed.info/sct?fhir_vs=ecl/{ecl_expression}",
        "count": 500
    }
    r = requests.get(FHIR_URL, params=params)
    r.raise_for_status()
    
    data = r.json() 
    # data.keys(): resourceType, url (=query), expansion

    # return list of concepts data["expansion"]["contains"]
    return data.get("expansion", {}).get("contains", [])


Simple Usage Example: retrieve a single concept


Code
disease = "73211009" # Diabetes mellitus

query = f"{disease}" # very basic ECL query
results = query_ecl(query)

pd.DataFrame(results)









system
code
display




0
http://snomed.info/sct
73211009
DM - Diabetes mellitus









Complex ECL queries
More complicated ECL queries can also be used to search and retrieve multiple (related) concepts and attributes from the Snomed Ontology, e.g.

concept: “{code}”
parents: “>{code}”
children: “<{code}”
more examples: ECL quick reference







ImportantOntology Task




Find the Snomed CT code for disease “Obesity”
Find the ECL expression to determine

all parents concept for disease “obesity”
all children diseases for disease “obesity”

Determine all attributes (“Due to”) for disease “Diabetes Mellitus”
Help

ECL help
Snowstorm Training. Browser & API




Code
disease="..."                # find code for "obesity"

# find ECL expression for 
parents  = query_ecl(f"...") # children (=direct descendants)
children = query_ecl(f"...") # parents (=direct ancestors)


print(f'Retrieved: {disease}')
print('Parents:  \n', pd.DataFrame(parents))
print('Children: \n', pd.DataFrame(children))

disease = "..."              # find code for Diabetes mellitus
attrib  = "..."              # find code for attribute "due to"
results = query_ecl(f"...")  # find query to extract attributes
print('Attributes: \n', pd.DataFrame(results))

title: Data Exploration
text:
Code
import requests

title: Data Exploration
text:
Code
import requests
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
from scipy.stats import ttest_ind, ttest_rel

title: Data Exploration
text:
Generate synthetic data
This data set will be used throughout this notebook.
The idea is to simulate the effect of a hypothetical drug treatment to lower HbA1c levels in patients with diabetes. We will then explore different ways to visualize the data and interpret the results.


Code
np.random.seed(42)
n = 30

# synthetic HbA1c-Werte [in %]
# normal 5%, ehoeht: 6%, Diabetes mellitus: > 6.5%, 8% Handlungsbedarf
before = np.random.normal(loc=9.0, scale=1, size=n)

# Two treatmentgroups
group = np.random.choice(["A", "B"], size=n, p=[0.25, 0.75])

after = before.copy()
after[group == "A"] -= np.random.normal(0.5, 0.1, size=(group == "A").sum())
after[group == "B"] += np.random.normal(0.0, 0.1, size=(group == "B").sum())

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "patient": np.arange(n),
    "before": before,
    "after": after,
    "group": group
})

df[["before","after"]].head()
#print(df[["before", "after"]].mean())
#df.groupby("group").describe().T









before
after




0
9.496714
9.594269


1
8.861736
8.813818


2
9.647689
9.215381


3
10.523030
10.504464


4
8.765847
8.204679













NoteQuestions




Why do we need to summarize this data?
Why do we need data visualization
Which tools?

title: Data Exploration
text:
Data Reshaping
For some visualization tasks it will be better to have data in a long format. We will use the melt function from pandas to reshape the data. I use a categorical time variable to distinguish before and after treatment.


Code
long = df.melt(
        id_vars=["patient"],
        value_vars=["before", "after"],
        var_name="time",
        value_name="hba1c"
    )

# define a consitent order for the time variable
ORDER = ["before", "after"]
long["time"] = pd.Categorical(
    long["time"],
    categories=ORDER,
    ordered=True
)

long.head()









patient
time
hba1c




0
0
before
9.496714


1
1
before
8.861736


2
2
before
9.647689


3
3
before
10.523030


4
4
before
8.765847

title: Data Exploration
text:
Barplots


Code
sns.barplot(data=long, x="time", y="hba1c", errorbar=None)
plt.ylim(8.6, 8.9)
plt.title("Clear effect on Mean")
plt.show()















CautionWhat is wrong with this plot ?








Code
sns.barplot(data=long, x="time", y="hba1c", errorbar=None)
plt.title("Almost no effect")
plt.show()









We tend to give meaning to barplot length, rather than position.
Until now we have focused on the mean values. But to discuss any potential treatment effect we will also have to analyse (and visualize) the variability.


Code
sns.barplot(data=long, x="time", y="hba1c", errorbar='se')
plt.title("Mean ± SE. Perhaps a small effect?")
plt.show()















NoteVariance, Standard Deviation & Standard Error





Variance Estimate: \[
\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^N (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \approx E[(X - \mu)^2]
\]
Standard Deviation (SD): \[
\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}
\]
Standard Error (SE): \[
SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}} \longrightarrow 0 \text{ for } N \rightarrow \infty
\]



The standard error (SE) of the mean is not a measure of variability in the data. For more data it will approach zero. The standard deviation (SD) is a better measure of variability in the data.


Code
sns.barplot(data=long, x="time", y="hba1c", errorbar='sd')
plt.title("Mean ± SD. Probably no effect.")
plt.show()











Code
# you are allowed to remove zero with boxplots
sns.boxplot(data=long, x="time", y="hba1c")
plt.title("Maybe some effect after all?")
plt.show()











Code
sns.violinplot(data=long, x="time", y="hba1c")
plt.title("Violin plot: Beware of smoothing artefacts!")
plt.show()









The violin plot employs some smoothing to visyualize the distribution. For small data sets this can be misleading. If possible, it is always better to show the data.
A statistical test can be used to support the visual impression.


Code
sns.stripplot( data=long, x="time", y="hba1c",
    # optional: switch off jitter
    jitter=False
)

t, p = ttest_ind( df["before"], df["after"])
title = f"Show data & test. p-value = {p:.5f}"
plt.title(title)
plt.show()









In the above we have assumed independent samples. But since we have repeated measures (before and after treatment) we should use a paired test. Here I also visualize the dependencies by connecting related data points explicitly with lines. This is only possible if we have a unique identifier for each patient (here: patient variable).


Code
sns.lineplot(data=long, x="time", y="hba1c",
    units="patient",
    estimator=None,
    marker="o"
)
t, p = ttest_rel( df["before"], df["after"])
title = f"Data Dependencies? p-value {p:.5f}"
plt.title(title)
plt.show()









There seems to be a significant reduction in HbA1c levels for some patients. Are there other variables that can explain this difference?


Code
long_group = df.melt(
        id_vars=["patient","group"],
        value_vars=["before", "after"],
        var_name="time",
        value_name="hba1c"
    )

ORDER = ["before", "after"]
long_group["time"] = pd.Categorical(
    long_group["time"],
    categories=ORDER,
    ordered=True
)

sns.lineplot(data=long_group, x="time", y="hba1c",
    units="patient",
    estimator=None,
    marker="o",
    hue="group"
)
t, p = ttest_rel( df["before"], df["after"])
title = f"Discover new variables"
plt.title(title)
plt.show()









But is this also a clinically relevant effect? We should always be careful to distinguish statistical significance from medical significance.






ImportantSummary




Don’t cut barplots !
Show variability in data (clarify SE or SD)
better show the range \(\to\) boxplots & violinplots
beware of smoothing violins (especially for small data sets)
show all the data if you can \(\to\) stripplot
multimodal distribution & additional group variables?
data exploration is visual and iterative
statistical significance \(\ne\) medical significance

import requests

FIGSIZE = (7, 4)

title: Data Visualization Tasks
text:
Code
import requests
import warnings
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns

FIGSIZE = (7, 4)

title: Data Visualization Tasks
text:
Data and Definitons


Code
from ucimlrepo import fetch_ucirepo 
  
dataset = fetch_ucirepo(id=544) 
print('keys: ', dataset.keys())  # data, metadata, variables
df = dataset.data.original 
cat_cols = df.select_dtypes(exclude='number').columns

print('categorical variables:', cat_cols)


keys:  dict_keys(['data', 'metadata', 'variables'])
categorical variables: Index(['Gender', 'family_history_with_overweight', 'FAVC', 'CAEC', 'SMOKE',
       'SCC', 'CALC', 'MTRANS', 'NObeyesdad'],
      dtype='object')

title: Data Visualization Tasks
text:
Task 1


Code
var1="NObeyesdad"
var2="Gender"
ct = pd.crosstab(df[var1], df[var2])
plt.figure(figsize=FIGSIZE)
sns.heatmap(ct, annot=True, fmt="d", cmap="RdYlGn")
plt.show()










Explain the problems with this plot
implement improvements to visualize the counts.
normalize the counts to visualize the joint distribution over the two variables
replace var1 with “SCC” (Monitoring Caloric Intake) and prepare a “Mosaic plot” to visualize the counts

title: Data Visualization Tasks
text:
Task 2: Pie2Bar
Marginal and conditional distributions for categorical variables are often visualized as Pie charts.
The following would visualize the proportions of obesity categories given a gender. \[
Pr(\mbox{Obesity Category}| \mbox{Gender})
\]


Code
var1, var2 = "Gender", "NObeyesdad"
tab = pd.crosstab(df[var1], df[var2], normalize="index")

# notice transposition tab.T
axes = tab.T.plot.pie(
    subplots=True,
    figsize=FIGSIZE,
    legend=False,
)
plt.show()





Conditional distributions of obesity by gender




However this is not a very good visual encoding.

Explain why pie charts are difficult to interpret and compare visually.
Replace the pie chart with a stacked barplot to compare the distribution of obesity category by Gender

title: Data Visualization Tasks
text:
Task 3: Risk Factors & UpSets
Several variables in the obesity dataset describe patient-level risk factors or behaviours. They can be encoded as Boolean variables and they define sets of individuals based on the Boolean status (True / False).


Code
from venn import venn

risk_vars = ["family_history_with_overweight", "FAVC", "SMOKE", "SCC"]
risk_bool = df[risk_vars].eq("yes") # convert to Boolean
risk_bool.head(3)

# construct sets of elements (= individuals)
elements = risk_bool.index # all indicies of risk_bool
venn_sets = {
    col: set(elements[risk_bool[col]])
    for col in risk_bool.columns
}

plt.figure(figsize=FIGSIZE)
venn(venn_sets)
plt.title("Venn Diagram")
plt.show()


<Figure size 1050x600 with 0 Axes>









Based on this Venn Diagram

Determine the total elements (individuals) in each set
How many individuals smoke and monitor their caloric intake (SCC=True)
Explain why a Venn diagram becomes hard to read when several sets are compared
Prepare an UpSet plot for the same analysis and sort by intersect size (cardinality)

You can use this package and data preparation


Code
# if needed
# !pip install upsetplot

from upsetplot import UpSet, from_indicators

warnings.filterwarnings(
    "ignore",
    category=FutureWarning,
    module="upsetplot"
)

# define multi-index of Booleans
upset_data = from_indicators(risk_bool.columns, risk_bool)
# count multi-index
upset_counts = upset_data.index.value_counts()

title: Data Visualization
text:
Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns

FIGSIZE = (7, 4)

title: Data Visualization
text:
Scenario
A health minister was told that hospital-associated infections are a major concern in health care. She orders a review of all hospitals who submit the conditions of all their current patients in standardized ICD-10 format.
The minister asks:

“Which hospital looks most worrying in this report?”


Group Challenge
Imagine you are the analyst team preparing a short briefing for the minister.
You have obtained a long table of patient-level data from all hospitals in the following format (first few rows shown)


Code
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)

# hospital sizes
N = {"A": 10000, "B": 5000, "C": 500}

# infection rates
p = {
    "A": 0.10,  
    "B": 0.12,
    "C": 0.12
}

dfs = []

for hospital, n in N.items():

    condition = rng.choice(
        ["Y95", "Other"],
        size=n,
        p=[p[hospital], 1 - p[hospital]]
    )

    dfs.append(
        pd.DataFrame({
            "hospital": hospital,
            "condition": condition
        })
    )

df = pd.concat(dfs, ignore_index=True)
df.head()









hospital
condition




0
A
Other


1
A
Other


2
A
Other


3
A
Other


4
A
Y95







For simplicity:

each patient has only 1 condition and is represented by 1 row
there are thousands of ICD codes, but we have summarized to only two levels: “Y95” and “Other”







ImportantGroup Task (5min)




What is the meaning for ICD-10 code “Y95” ?
What type of variables are available in the data
How should we summarize the finding?

title: Data Visualization
text:
Base line - prevalence
First we would probably want to have a baseline of


Code
condition_table = df["condition"].value_counts(dropna=True)
print(condition_table)


condition
Other    13887
Y95       1613
Name: count, dtype: int64


Notice: for simplicity, in this example there are only two conditions (“Y95” and “Other”). In general there would be many more conditions.
It might be easier to report this as proportions


Code
condition_table = df["condition"].value_counts(dropna=True, normalize=True)
print(condition_table)


condition
Other    0.895935
Y95      0.104065
Name: proportion, dtype: float64


Notice: This is called the marginal distribution, because we ignore the variable hospital.
Jargon: summed over (=marginalized) over all hospitals

title: Data Visualization
text:
Conditional Distribution
The minister (and we) suspect a different number of patients with condition=“Y95” in different hopsitals. Let’s first filter the data for the condition of interest.


Code
df_sub = df[df["condition"] == "Y95"]
df_sub.head()









hospital
condition




4
A
Y95


17
A
Y95


27
A
Y95


51
A
Y95


68
A
Y95







Jargon: Such a filter step is also called conditioning
Now let’s summarize this subset of patient-wise data per hospital (and normalize)


Code
# summarize by counting
count_summary = df_sub["hospital"].value_counts(dropna=True, normalize=True)
print("Conditional Distribution:\n", count_summary)


Conditional Distribution:
 hospital
A    0.610043
B    0.352139
C    0.037818
Name: proportion, dtype: float64




Code
# This answers P(hospital|condition)
count_summary.plot.pie(autopct="%1.1f%%")
# bars and stacked bars would be better still
# count_summary.plot(kind="bar")
# count_summary.to_frame().T.plot(kind="bar", stacked=True)
plt.title('Conditional Distribution (condition = Y95)')
plt.show()















ImportantQuestions




Should we just report this plot to the minister?
Which hospital is the worst?

title: Data Visualization
text:
Marginal Distribution
Just like with the prior probability for condition we should also take into account the number of patients per hospital. This is equivalent to summing (marginalizing) over all conditions
\[
Pr(\mbox{Hospital}) = \sum_{\mbox{condition}} Pr(\mbox{Hospital, condition})
\]


Code
marg_summary = df["hospital"].value_counts()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=FIGSIZE)
count_summary.plot.pie(autopct="%1.1f%%", ax=ax[0])
marg_summary.plot.pie(autopct="%1.1f%%", ax=ax[1])
ax[0].set_title('Conditional Distribution \n (cond="Y95")')
ax[1].set_title('Marginal Distribution \n (cond="All)')
plt.show()





Marginal Distributions change our expectation










NoteConditional Challenges




Marginal distributions define our expectations: more patients \(\to\) more diseases
P(Hosptial|Condition) \(\ne\) Pr(Condition|Hospital)





Code
# this will only calculate row margins, since each column (hospital) is normalized to 1
tab = pd.crosstab(df["condition"], df["hospital"], 
    normalize="columns", margins=True)
fig, axes = plt.subplots( 2, 2, figsize=(8, 8))
axes = axes.flatten()

for ax, col in zip(axes, tab.columns):
    tab[col].plot.pie(
        ax=ax,
        legend=False,
        explode=[0.1, 0.0]
    )
    ax.set_title(col)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





P(condition|Hospital)










NotePie Challenges




not good for many categories
not good for comparisons (areas)

title: Data Visualization
text:
Joint Distribution
Conditional Distributions are confusing. Let’s try to visualize all proportions at once


Code
ct = pd.crosstab(df["hospital"], df["condition"], normalize="all")
plt.figure(figsize=FIGSIZE)
sns.heatmap(ct, annot=True, fmt=".3f", cmap="RdYlGn")
plt.show()















ImportantQuestion



What is the problem with this visualization?



Colour-encoding of numbers has to be done carefully.
Consider colour blindness, meaning of divergent colours?
area has no meaning



Code
plt.figure(figsize=FIGSIZE)
sns.heatmap(ct, annot=True, fmt=".3f", cmap="Blues")
plt.show()





Better choice of colours




Another useful encoding can be by area size (Mosaic plot)


Code
from statsmodels.graphics.mosaicplot import mosaic

#plt.figure(figsize=FIGSIZE)
mosaic(df, index=["hospital", "condition"], gap=0.01, title="Mosaic Plot")
plt.show()





Area-encoding can also be useful










TipJoint and Margins





Sometimes it is useful to show the joint distributions with the margins


Code
jdm = pd.crosstab(df["hospital"], df["condition"], normalize="all", margins=True)
print('Joint Distribution with Margins: \n', jdm)


Joint Distribution with Margins: 
 condition     Other       Y95       All
hospital                               
A          0.581677  0.063484  0.645161
B          0.285935  0.036645  0.322581
C          0.028323  0.003935  0.032258
All        0.895935  0.104065  1.000000


Based on the margins we can also calculate the expected proportion of in a given cell.
For example:
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
Pr(\mbox{Hospital=A and Condition=Y95}) = \\
Pr(\mbox{Hospital=A}) * Pr(\mbox{Condition=Y95}) = \\
0.645 * 0.104 = 0.06708\\
\end{array}
\]

title: Data Visualization
text:
Final Suggestion


Code
# this will only calculate column margins, since all rows (index) will be normalized to 1
tab = pd.crosstab(df["hospital"], df["condition"], margins=True, normalize="index")
tab.plot.bar(stacked=True)
plt.legend(bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1), title="Conditions")
plt.title('Figure for Minister')
plt.tight_layout()





Barplots enable better comparisons










ImportantConclusions



Data Analysis shows that the hopsital-associated infection (“Y95”) are very similar across hospitals and similar to the overall average rate. In hospital “C” there is a slightly higher level. It remains to be seen if this is “statistical significant”.

title: Data Visualization
text:
Breaking News
A newspaper heard about our study and got access to the same data. The following article was published the next day:






WarningShocking Revelation



A confidential goverment report found that hospital “B” has much higher rate of infections than the national average. The health minister did not respond to our questions.


Code
tab_sub = 100*tab.drop(columns=["A","C"], index="Other")
ax = tab_sub.T.plot.bar(legend=None, color="grey")
ax.set_ylim([10,11.5])
ax.set_yticks([10, 10.5, 11])

ax.set_ylabel("Infection Rate")
ax.patches[0].set_color("darkorange")
plt.title('Scandal at Hospital B: worse than National Average!!')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

















ImportantQuestion



How would you comment on this article?

title: Data Visualization
text:
Summary

Visualization is important
Visualization can mislead
Conditioning can be confusing
Marginal Models set expectations
Avoid Pie charts
Need statistical model

title: Data Visualization
text:
Python Firsts


Code
print(f"df.shape {df.shape}")
print(f"df.descibe \n{df.describe()}")


df.shape (15500, 2)
df.descibe 
       hospital condition
count     15500     15500
unique        3         2
top           A     Other
freq      10000     13887


… this is important for data management but not for the minster.

title: Data Visualization
text:
Prevalence - a base line
First we would probably want to have a baseline of


Code
condition_table = df["condition"].value_counts(dropna=True)
print(condition_table)


condition
Other    13887
Y95       1613
Name: count, dtype: int64


It might be easier to report this as proportions


Code
condition_table = df["condition"].value_counts(dropna=True, normalize=True)
print(condition_table)


condition
Other    0.895935
Y95      0.104065
Name: proportion, dtype: float64


Notice: This is called the marginal distribution, because we ignore the variable hospital.
Jargon: summed over (=marginalized) over all hospitals

title: Data Visualization
text:
Prior Distribution - a baseline
First we would probably want to have a baseline of proportions (regardless of hospital)


Code
condition_table = df["condition"].value_counts(dropna=True)
print(condition_table)


condition
Other    13887
Y95       1613
Name: count, dtype: int64


Jargon:
Because we ignore the variable hospital this amounts to summing over all hospitals (= marginalization)
\[
N(\mbox{Condition}) = \sum_{\mbox{Hospital}} N(\mbox{Hospital, Condition})
\]
Rather than numbers, it might be easier to report this as proportions


Code
condition_table = df["condition"].value_counts(dropna=True, normalize=True)
print(condition_table)


condition
Other    0.895935
Y95      0.104065
Name: proportion, dtype: float64


So the prior probability for condition=“Y95” is about 10%.

title: Data Visualization
text:
Suggestion


Code
tab.T.plot.bar(stacked=True)
plt.legend(bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1), title="Conditions")
plt.title('Figure for Minister')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





Barplots for clearer comparisons and communication










NoteConclusions



Data analysis shows that the rates of hospital-associated infection (ICD code “Y95”) are very similar across hospitals and similar to the overall average rate. In hospital “C” there is a slightly higher level.

title: Data Visualization
text:
Appendix: Joint Distributions
Let’s try to visualize all proportions at once


Code
ct = pd.crosstab(df["hospital"], df["condition"], normalize="all")
plt.figure(figsize=FIGSIZE)
sns.heatmap(ct, annot=True, fmt=".3f", cmap="RdYlGn")
plt.show()















ImportantQuestion



What is the problem with this visualization?



Colour-encoding of numbers has to be done carefully.
Consider colour blindness, meaning of divergent colours?
area has no meaning



Code
plt.figure(figsize=FIGSIZE)
sns.heatmap(ct, annot=True, fmt=".3f", cmap="Blues")
plt.show()





Better choice of colours




Another useful encoding can be by area size (Mosaic plot)


Code
from statsmodels.graphics.mosaicplot import mosaic

#plt.figure(figsize=FIGSIZE)
mosaic(df, index=["hospital", "condition"], gap=0.01, title="Mosaic Plot")
plt.show()





Area-encoding can also be useful










TipJoint and Margins





Sometimes it is useful to show the joint distributions with the margins


Code
jdm = pd.crosstab(df["hospital"], df["condition"], normalize="all", margins=True)
print('Joint Distribution with Margins: \n', jdm)


Joint Distribution with Margins: 
 condition     Other       Y95       All
hospital                               
A          0.581677  0.063484  0.645161
B          0.285935  0.036645  0.322581
C          0.028323  0.003935  0.032258
All        0.895935  0.104065  1.000000


Based on the margins and a model (independence of variables!) we can also calculate the expected proportion of in a given cell.
For example:
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
Pr(\mbox{Hospital=A, Condition=Y95}) = \\
Pr(\mbox{Hospital=A}) * Pr(\mbox{Condition=Y95}) = \\
0.645 * 0.104 = 0.06708 > 0.645161 \\
\end{array}
\]

title: Data Visualization
text:
Prior Distributions - baselines
First we would want to have a baseline for conditions (regardless of hospital)


Code
condition_table = df["condition"].value_counts(dropna=True)
print(condition_table)


condition
Other    13887
Y95       1613
Name: count, dtype: int64


Jargon:
Because we ignore the variable hospital this amounts to summing each condition over all hospitals (= marginalization)
\[
N(\mbox{Condition}) = \sum_{\mbox{Hospital}} N(\mbox{Hospital, Condition})
\]
Rather than numbers, it might be easier to report this as proportions


Code
condition_marginal = df["condition"].value_counts(dropna=True, normalize=True)
print(condition_marginal)


condition
Other    0.895935
Y95      0.104065
Name: proportion, dtype: float64


So the prior probability for condition=“Y95” is about 10%. In the disease context this is also called “prevelance”.
And similarly for Hospitals:
\[
N(\mbox{Hospital}) = \sum_{\mbox{Condition}} N(\mbox{Hospital, Condition})
\]


Code
hospital_table = df["hospital"].value_counts(dropna=True, normalize=True)
print(hospital_table)


hospital
A    0.645161
B    0.322581
C    0.032258
Name: proportion, dtype: float64








ImportantQuestion



How to visualize proportions that sum to 100% ?




Code
fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=FIGSIZE)
condition_table.plot.pie(autopct="%1.1f%%", ax=ax[0])
hospital_table.plot.pie(autopct="%1.1f%%", ax=ax[1])
ax[0].set_title('Conditions - Marginals')
ax[1].set_title('Hospitals - Marginals')
plt.show()





Visualizations are better than tables

title: Data Visualization

title: Data Visualization
text:
Conditionals and Priors


Code
marg_summary = df["hospital"].value_counts()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=FIGSIZE)
count_summary.plot.pie(autopct="%1.1f%%", ax=ax[0])
marg_summary.plot.pie(autopct="%1.1f%%", ax=ax[1])
ax[0].set_title('Conditional Distribution \n (cond="Y95")')
ax[1].set_title('Prior Distribution \n (cond="All)')
plt.show()





Prior distributions affect our expectations










NoteConditional Challenges




Prior (marginal) distributions define our expectations: more patients \(\to\) more diseases
P(Hospital|Condition) \(\ne\) Pr(Condition|Hospital)





Code
# this will only calculate row margins, since each column (hospital) is normalized to 1
tab = pd.crosstab(df["condition"], df["hospital"], 
    normalize="columns", margins=True)
fig, axes = plt.subplots( 2, 2, figsize=(8, 8))
axes = axes.flatten()

for ax, col in zip(axes, tab.columns):
    tab[col].plot.pie(
        ax=ax,
        legend=False,
        explode=[0.1, 0.0],
        ylabel=""
    )
    ax.set_title(col)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





P(condition|Hospital)










NotePie Challenges




not good for many categories
not good for comparisons (areas)
